title: Valid Plane Trees: Combinatorial Models for RNA Secondary Structures with Watson-Crick Base Pairs
overall_summary: The paper studies rooted plane trees whose boundary half-edges are labeled by a word P in a complementary alphabet, with the constraint that every tree edge pairs complementary letters; equivalently, these are noncrossing perfect matchings of the letters of P. Its main structural result is that the graph of P-valid plane trees is connected under valid local moves of Condon-Heitsch-Hoos, and after orienting local moves of type 2 this graph is acyclic with a unique sink. The unique sink is produced by an explicit greedy stack algorithm and is also characterized by having no outgoing type-2 move and by minimizing total distance from the root. The core techniques are the plane-tree/noncrossing-matching dictionary, interval decomposition along an edge, a leaf argument that creates a common edge by one valid local move, and a Lyapunov function for directed local moves. For the original problem, the paper is most useful for understanding the 0- and 1-dimensional combinatorics: noncrossing inverse-letter matchings are exactly valid plane trees, and the boundary of a one-crossing matching corresponds closely to the paper's two local resolutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.1 (bijection between plane trees and noncrossing matchings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a rooted plane tree with n edges have its 2n half-edges labeled 1,2,...,2n by walking counterclockwise from the root, and write e(i,j) for the edge whose left and right half-edge labels are i and j. There is a classical bijection between rooted plane trees with n edges and noncrossing perfect matchings on the set {1,...,2n}, sending each edge e(i,j) to the matched pair (i,j). This bijection respects inclusion: subtrees of a plane tree correspond to submatchings of the noncrossing matching, and conversely. If P=p1p2...p2n is a word in a complementary alphabet and a plane tree is P-valid, meaning that p_i and p_j are complements whenever e(i,j) is an edge, then the associated noncrossing perfect matching pairs only complementary letters of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Reading the two half-edge labels of every edge in a rooted plane tree gives n pairs on {1,...,2n}; planarity of the embedded rooted tree prevents pairs i< i' < j < j' from occurring, so the matching is noncrossing. Conversely, a noncrossing perfect matching can be thickened or nested in the standard Catalan way to recover a rooted plane tree whose counterclockwise boundary labels are the original endpoints. The inclusion statement follows because an edge e(i,j) cuts out a contiguous interval of boundary labels inside it, and the pairs entirely in that interval are exactly the corresponding subtree or submatching. The validity assertion is immediate from the definition of P-validity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 (basic structure of plane trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a rooted plane tree with n edges, with half-edges labeled 1,2,...,2n counterclockwise from the root, and let e(i,j) denote an edge with left half-edge label i and right half-edge label j. Then: (1) S satisfies the noncrossing condition: no two edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') have i < i' < j < j'. (2) For each edge e(i,j), the half-edges whose labels lie strictly between i and j form a subtree, and the half-edges whose labels lie outside the interval [i,j] form the complementary subtree. (3) For every edge e(i,j), the labels i and j have opposite parity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The noncrossing condition is the usual planar/noncrossing property under the standard bijection between plane trees and noncrossing matchings. Once noncrossing is known, an edge e(i,j) separates the boundary labels into the interval i+1,...,j-1 and its complement; no edge can have one endpoint in the interval and one endpoint outside without crossing e(i,j), so these labels form subtrees. Since the interval between i and j is filled by complete edges, it contains an even number of half-edge labels; hence j-i-1 is even, so j-i is odd and i,j have opposite parity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.2 and Propositions 2.6-2.7 (valid local moves and endpoint-label criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, meaning a set with a fixed-point-free involution B ↦ bar(B), and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. A plane tree S is P-valid if every edge e(i,j) of S has p_j=bar(p_i). For indices i<j<i'<j', a type-1 local move replaces two adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') of a plane tree by e(i,j') and e(j,i'); a type-2 local move is the inverse operation, replacing adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') by e(i,j) and e(i',j'). A local move is valid with respect to P if both the initial and final plane trees are P-valid. A type-1 local move is valid if and only if its inverse type-2 local move is valid. More explicitly, a type-1 move on adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') is valid if and only if there is a letter B in A such that p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B). Similarly, a type-2 move on adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') is valid if and only if there is a letter B in A such that p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B). Consequently the graph G_P whose vertices are P-valid plane trees and whose edges are valid local moves is a well-defined undirected graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The two local moves are inverse as operations on plane trees, so validity of one is equivalent to validity of the inverse between the same two P-valid trees. For the endpoint-label criterion, consider type 1. Since the initial tree is P-valid, p_i is complementary to p_j and p_i' is complementary to p_j'. The final tree is P-valid exactly when p_i is complementary to p_j' and p_j is complementary to p_i'. Complements are unique in A, so p_j and p_j' must be the same complement of p_i, and p_i and p_i' must be the same complement of p_j. Thus p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B) for some B. Conversely, those equalities make all four possible pairings complementary, so both initial and final trees are valid. The type-2 statement is the same argument applied to the inverse move. Since every valid move has a valid inverse, the graph with edges given by valid local moves is undirected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8 (validity inherited by interval subtrees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A, and let T be a P-valid rooted plane tree. Suppose T contains an edge e(i,j), and let v be the endpoint of e(i,j) farthest from the root. Let T_in be the subgraph consisting of e(i,j) together with all descendants of v; its half-edge labels are exactly i,i+1,...,j. Let P_in be the subsequence of P in those positions. Let T_out be the subgraph obtained by erasing T_in from T, and let P_out be the subsequence of P in the complementary positions. Then T_in is P_in-valid and T_out is P_out-valid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the convention for labeling half-edges of a rooted plane tree, the descendants of the far endpoint v of e(i,j) are exactly the half-edges with labels i+1,...,j-1. These descendants form a subtree, and adding e(i,j) gives T_in. Every edge of T_in is an edge of T, so its endpoint labels are complementary in P and hence also in the restricted sequence P_in. Removing T_in leaves the connected outside subgraph T_out with the outside half-edge labels; its edges are again inherited from the P-valid tree T, so they remain complementary with respect to P_out.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.9 (one valid local move can create a common edge)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A, and let T and T' be two P-valid plane trees. Then either T and T' share an edge, or there exists a valid local move on one of the trees, say T' to T'', such that T and T'' share an edge.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume T and T' have no common edge. Choose a leaf edge of T. A leaf edge has consecutive half-edge labels, so write it as e(i,i+1). Since T is P-valid, the letters p_i and p_{i+1} are complementary. In T', the half-edges i and i+1 cannot be paired to each other, because T and T' share no edge. The noncrossing/plane-tree structure forces the two edges incident to the half-edges i and i+1 in T' to occur in one of three adjacent local configurations: both i and i+1 are left half-edges, both are right half-edges, or they form a peak-shaped nested configuration. Let the other endpoints in these two edges be j and k. Because T' is P-valid and p_i,p_{i+1} are complementary, the labels at j and k have exactly the complementary pattern required by the endpoint-label criterion for a valid local move. In each of the three configurations, performing the appropriate type-1 or type-2 local move replaces the two adjacent edges by a configuration containing the leaf e(i,i+1). The resulting tree T'' is P-valid and shares the edge e(i,i+1) with T.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.2 and Lemma 3.3 (greedy stack algorithm and interval deletion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. The greedy algorithm reads the letters from left to right using a stack of indexed letters: push p1; for each subsequent letter p_j, inspect the top indexed letter p_i on the stack, and if p_i=bar(p_j), pop p_i and record the ordered pair (i,j), while otherwise pushing p_j. Let E(P) be the set of recorded pairs. If the algorithm, when run on P, pops p_i at the jth step, and if P' is obtained from P by deleting the contiguous block p_i p_{i+1} ... p_j while retaining the original indices outside that block, then running the greedy algorithm on P' gives the same final stack as running it on P. Moreover E(P) is the disjoint union of E(P') and a perfect matching M on the set of indices {i,i+1,...,j}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p_i is popped at step j, then p_i must still be on top of the stack immediately before reading p_j. Therefore every letter among p_{i+1},...,p_{j-1} that was ever pushed onto the stack must have been popped before step j; otherwise it would lie above p_i and prevent p_i from being popped. Also no letter among p_1,...,p_{i-1} can have been popped during the intervening steps i+1,...,j-1, because p_i remains above them until step j. Thus the operations between i and j match all indices in the interval {i,...,j} among themselves, and after step j the stack is exactly what it was just before p_i was read. Deleting the interval therefore does not affect the subsequent stack evolution outside the interval, and the recorded pairs split into the outside pairs E(P') and the perfect matching M inside the deleted interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4 (the greedy algorithm finds a valid plane tree whenever one exists)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. If there exists at least one P-valid plane tree, then the greedy stack algorithm terminates with an empty stack, and its recorded pairs E(P) form a P-valid plane tree T0(P).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The interval-deletion lemma implies that whenever the greedy algorithm records a pair (i,j), all indices between i and j are matched among themselves before p_i is popped; hence the recorded pairs are nested/noncrossing. Thus an empty final stack gives a P-valid noncrossing perfect matching, equivalently a P-valid plane tree. It remains to prove the stack is empty if any valid tree exists. The paper argues by minimal counterexample. Suppose Q=q1...q2k is a shortest word for which a Q-valid plane tree exists but the greedy algorithm ends with a nonempty stack. Any valid plane tree has a leaf e(i,i+1), so q_i and q_{i+1} are complementary; if no pop has occurred before step i+1, then the algorithm pops at that step, and otherwise it already popped earlier. Hence the greedy algorithm pops at least once. Let the first pop occur at step j+1, necessarily popping q_j, so q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary. By Lemma 3.3, deleting q_j q_{j+1} gives a shorter word Q' on which the greedy algorithm has the same final nonempty stack. If some Q-valid tree has the leaf e(j,j+1), remove that leaf to obtain a Q'-valid tree. If not, take any Q-valid tree; the two consecutive half-edges j and j+1 lie in one of the adjacent local configurations from Proposition 2.9, and because q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary, a valid local move produces a Q-valid tree containing the leaf e(j,j+1), which can then be removed. Thus Q' is a shorter word with a valid tree but nonempty greedy stack, contradicting minimality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5 (the greedy tree has no valid type-2 move)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T0(P) be the P-valid tree produced by the greedy algorithm. Then T0(P) has no valid local move of type 2; equivalently, it has no adjacent nested pair of edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') with i<j<i'<j' that can be validly replaced by e(i,j) and e(i',j').
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose such a valid type-2 move existed in T0(P). By the endpoint-label criterion, for some letter B one has p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B), after possibly naming B appropriately. In the greedy construction of T0(P), when the algorithm reaches position j, the letter p_i is still on the stack because it is paired in T0(P) with the later position j'. Proposition 2.8, applied to the relevant subtrees inside the edge e(i,j'), ensures that all letters pushed after i and before j have already been popped, so p_i is actually on top of the stack at step j. Since p_j is its complement, the greedy algorithm would pop p_i at step j and create the edge e(i,j), contradicting the assumption that T0(P) contains e(i,j') instead. Therefore no valid type-2 move exists in T0(P).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6 (every non-greedy valid tree has an outgoing type-2 move)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T0(P) be the greedy P-valid tree. If T is a P-valid plane tree with T ≠ T0(P), then T admits a valid local move of type 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since a noncrossing matching is determined by which half-edges are left endpoints and which are right endpoints, T and T0 differ at some first half-edge label j where one has j as a left half-edge and the other has j as a right half-edge. First show that j must be a right half-edge in T0. If instead j were a left half-edge in T0 and a right half-edge in T, let e(i',j) be the edge of T ending at j. The interval i'+1,...,j-1 forms a subtree of T, and by the choice of j all earlier left/right endpoint statuses agree with T0, so the same interval structure occurs in T0. Thus p_i' would be on top of the greedy stack at step j, and since T is P-valid, p_j is its complement; the greedy algorithm would pair i' with j, making j a right half-edge in T0 after all, a contradiction. Hence j is a right half-edge in T0, say in the edge e(i,j). Then i and j are both left half-edges in T: i is before the first difference and is left in T0, while j differs. The labels p_i and p_j are complementary because e(i,j) is an edge of T0. The interval between i and j is a subtree in T0 and, again by the first-difference choice, is also present in T. This gives exactly the local nested configuration in T that supports a type-2 move, and the endpoint-label criterion shows that the move is valid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7 (connectedness of the graph of valid plane trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. Let G_P be the graph whose vertices are the P-valid plane trees and whose edges are valid local moves of type 1 or type 2. If V(P), the set of P-valid plane trees, is nonempty, then G_P is connected; equivalently, any two P-valid plane trees can be joined by a finite sequence of valid local moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n, the number of edges. The case n=1 is immediate. Let S and T be two P-valid plane trees with n edges. If they share no edge, Proposition 2.9 gives a valid local move on one of them to a tree that shares an edge with the other; it is therefore enough to handle the case in which S and T share an edge e(i,j). Cut both trees along e(i,j). The portion inside the interval [i,j] and the complementary outside portion are P-valid trees for the corresponding shorter subsequences by Proposition 2.8. By the induction hypothesis, the inside subtree of S can be changed into the inside subtree of T by valid local moves supported entirely inside the interval, and the outside subtree of S can be changed into the outside subtree of T by valid local moves supported entirely outside the interval. Performing those same moves in the original tree, while keeping the common edge e(i,j) fixed, gives a path from S to T in G_P. Prepending the one move supplied by Proposition 2.9 handles the no-common-edge case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.8 (unique sink of the type-2 orientation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T0(P) be the greedy P-valid plane tree. Define a directed graph G_P^+ on the P-valid plane trees by directing an edge T → T' when T' is obtained from T by a valid local move of type 2. Then T0(P) is the unique sink of G_P^+.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Corollary 3.5 says that T0(P) has no valid type-2 move, so no edge of G_P^+ leaves T0(P); hence T0(P) is a sink. Theorem 3.6 says that every other P-valid tree has at least one valid type-2 move, so every other vertex has an outgoing directed edge. Therefore no other sink exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.10 (acyclicity and directed paths to the greedy tree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, let T0(P) be the greedy tree, and let G_P^+ be the directed graph whose edges are valid type-2 moves. Then G_P^+ has no directed cycles. Moreover, for every P-valid plane tree T, there is a directed path in G_P^+ from T to T0(P), consisting only of valid type-2 moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a rooted plane tree T with root v0, define d_T = sum over vertices v of dist(v,v0), the total distance of all vertices from the root. A direct comparison of the local type-2 move shows that d_T drops by at least one under every valid type-2 move, and drops by more if one of the intervening subtrees in the schematic is nonempty. Since d_T strictly decreases along directed edges, G_P^+ has no directed cycle. The undirected graph G_P is connected by Theorem 3.7 and finite. Starting from any T, repeatedly follow an outgoing edge if one exists. Strict decrease of d_T prevents infinite repetition, so the process terminates at a sink. By Corollary 3.8 the only sink is T0(P), giving a directed path from T to T0(P).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.11 (characterizations of the greedy tree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree. For a P-valid plane tree T, define d_T = sum over vertices v of dist(v,v0), where v0 is the root. The distinguished tree T0(P) may be characterized equivalently as the tree produced by the greedy stack algorithm, as the unique sink in the type-2 oriented graph G_P^+, and as a P-valid plane tree with minimum total distance d_T among all P-valid plane trees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence between the greedy construction and the unique sink statement is Corollary 3.8 together with the definition of T0(P). For the distance characterization, Corollary 3.10 supplies a directed path of type-2 moves from any P-valid tree T to T0(P), and each type-2 move strictly decreases d_T. Hence no tree reached before T0(P) on such a path can have smaller distance than T0(P), so T0(P) realizes the minimum of d_T over V(P).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 (every plane tree can occur as a greedy tree for some word)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet with 2m letters, m≥1. For every rooted plane tree T with n edges, there exists a word P=p1p2...p2n in A such that T is P-valid and T is the greedy tree T0(P) produced by the greedy algorithm.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Label each edge incident to the root by any complementary pair of letters from A. Every non-root edge has a parent edge. Proceed outward from the root, and label each non-root edge by a complementary pair chosen so that this edge and its parent do not form the endpoint-label pattern that would allow a valid type-2 local move. At each step, only one choice of the left half-edge label is forbidden by the parent, so at least 2m-1 choices remain. After all edges are labeled, the resulting labeled tree is P-valid for the word P read around the half-edges, and it has no valid type-2 local move because any type-2 move would occur between a parent edge and a child edge. By Theorem 3.6 or Corollary 3.11, a P-valid tree with no type-2 move must be the greedy tree T0(P).

proof_techniques.name: Plane-tree/noncrossing-matching dictionary
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces rooted plane trees by noncrossing perfect matchings on the boundary labels 1,...,2n. Each edge e(i,j) is a matched pair, and planarity is encoded by the absence of interleavings i<i'<j<j'. The dictionary also turns an edge e(i,j) into an interval decomposition: labels between i and j belong to the descendant subtree, while labels outside form the complementary part.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The vertices of F_w are 0-crossing matchings, i.e. noncrossing perfect matchings pairing inverse letters. This dictionary allows one to import rooted-tree language, interval subproblems, and local-move arguments directly at the vertex level.

proof_techniques.name: Stack-greedy normal form
proof_techniques.description: The greedy algorithm reads the word left to right, pushing a letter unless it is complementary to the stack top, in which case the stack top is popped and paired with the current letter. The key lemma says that if a pop pairs i with j, then the entire interval i,...,j is internally matched and can be deleted without changing the final outside stack. A minimal-counterexample argument then proves that if any valid plane tree exists, the greedy algorithm produces one.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This supplies a canonical noncrossing inverse-letter matching whenever such matchings exist. A contraction or discrete Morse strategy for F_w could try to flow all cells toward the greedy vertex, using the interval-deletion property to define recursive choices.

proof_techniques.name: Endpoint-label criterion for valid local resolutions
proof_techniques.description: For two adjacent edges with boundary labels i<j<i'<j', the paper identifies exactly when both local resolutions are valid: the two left endpoints must carry the same letter B and the two right endpoints must carry the complement bar(B). This reduces validity of a local move to a four-letter test independent of the surrounding subtrees.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A 1-crossing cell of F_w has two local resolutions. This criterion is the precise combinatorial test for when the two resolutions remain inverse-letter matchings, so it can identify the 1-skeleton moves induced by cell attachments.

proof_techniques.name: Create a common edge by moving a leaf
proof_techniques.description: To compare two valid plane trees T and T', the paper chooses a leaf e(i,i+1) of T. If T' does not contain this leaf, the consecutive half-edges i and i+1 occur in T' in one of three possible adjacent configurations. Since the leaf in T proves p_i and p_{i+1} are complementary, the endpoint-label criterion makes the appropriate local move in T' valid, and that move creates the desired leaf.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to force agreement with a target noncrossing matching after at most one local resolution move. It is a natural ingredient for inductive connectivity or contractibility arguments that cut along a shared arc.

proof_techniques.name: Cut along a common edge and recurse
proof_techniques.description: Once two valid trees share an edge e(i,j), Proposition 2.8 splits each tree into inside and outside valid subtrees for shorter subsequences. Local moves supported inside the interval and outside the interval can then be performed independently while keeping e(i,j) fixed. This is the induction step in the connectedness proof.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a subcomplex of F_w consisting of matchings containing a fixed noncrossing arc decomposes into smaller word-complexes, this technique suggests a recursive proof of contractibility or collapsibility.

proof_techniques.name: Acyclic orientation via total root distance
proof_techniques.description: The paper orients every valid local move according to type 2 and defines d_T as the sum of distances from the root to all vertices of a plane tree T. A direct local check shows every type-2 move strictly lowers d_T, so the directed graph has no cycles. Together with connectedness and the no-type-2 characterization of the greedy tree, this gives a unique sink and directed paths from every valid tree to that sink.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a candidate Lyapunov function for a discrete Morse or gradient-flow approach to F_w. One could try to extend the orientation from vertices/edges to higher-dimensional crossing cells by resolving crossings in the direction that decreases an analogue of d_T.

proof_techniques.name: Labeling to avoid type-2 moves
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.1 labels any prescribed plane tree so that no parent-child pair of edges satisfies the endpoint-label pattern for a type-2 move. The resulting tree is valid for the word read around it and, because it has no type-2 move, is the greedy tree for that word.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful for constructing controlled examples: one can prescribe the canonical noncrossing matching and then choose labels so that it is the unique sink of the valid local-move orientation.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that local moves need not involve successively labeled half-edges: there may be many other edges or subtrees between the four displayed labels, so locality is in the plane tree rather than in the boundary word. Remark 3.9 notes that the type-2 oriented graph can have multiple sources even though it has a unique sink; the example P=AABBAABB has two sources and one sink. The local moves used are those of Condon, Heitsch, and Hoos, and the paper cites Kemp-Mahlburg-Rattan-Smyth and Schumacher-Yan for related enumeration of noncrossing pairings on words.
why_relevant: Introduces foldings of complementary words around plane trees, equivalently noncrossing perfect matchings of inverse/complementary letters. This is directly relevant to the vertices of F_w and to greedy or inductive constructions of valid matchings.
